title: practical english usage - Part 282
content:
343 -ise    and   -ize ment, which was...

...is not completely in accordance with the controversial policy of the present govern­ or printed lines. (To see where to divide words, look in a good dictionary.) We   also use hyphens to separate the parts of long words at the end of written 4 word    division twenty-one thirty-six two-thirds e  numbers 21-99; fractions un-American re-examine counter-revolution in order to avoid unusual or misleading combinations of letters.

In both British and American English, all prefixes may be separated by hyphens ex-husband  post-publication   self-study co-producer  non-involvement  pro-hunting anti-war   mid-term      pre-meeting combinations are generally written as single words (e.g. antiwar).

are often separated from what follows by hyphens. In American English these In British English, the prefixes anti-, co-, ex-, mid-, non-, pre-, post-, pro- and self- d  prefixes role-play house-hunt c  compound verbs beginning with a noun They were ordered to shoot to kill.

- a shoot-to-kill policy He's out of work.

- an out-of-work miner.

When we  use a longer phrase as an adjective before a noun, we  often use grey-green broken-hearted red-hot nice-looking blue-eyed b  compound adjectives Word  Formation and SpellingSection 30 vocabulary •  344   -able and -ible public -> publicly Exception: tragic -> tragically phonetic -> phonetically If an adjective ends in -ic, the adverb ends in -ically (pronounced /ɪkli/).

4 adjectives        ending     in  -ic idle -> idly noble -> nobly able -» ably -le changes to -ly after a consonant.

3  adjectives        ending      in  consonant        + /e shy -> shyly sly -> slyly coy -» coyly Exceptions: easy -> easily    gay -> gaily or happy -> happily dry -> drily   dryly -y usually changes to -i- (►  348).

due -> duly full -> fully true -> truly whole -> wholly Exceptions: not complete •»  completely (     eompleffy) not right -> rightly  real -> really (     reafy)  pale -» palely late -> lately   hopefill -> hopefully    definite -> definitely We   normally change an adjective into an adverb by adding -ly.

1  adverb      formation 345 -ly e.g. inaudible, irresponsible.

Negatives of these words are naturally also spelt with -ible: edible     indelible resistible divisible   horrible  possible  visible digestible   gullible   plausible terrible defensible  forcible  permissibletangible credible    flexible  perceptiblesusceptible convertible  feasible  negligible suggestible comprehensiblefallible  legible   sensible audible    exhaustibleinvincible reversible accessible  eligible  intelligibleresponsible (Both are pronounced the same: /əbl/). Some common words spelt with -ible: The  suffix -able, as in readable, is far more common than -ible, as in audible.

344 -able     and   -ible For American English, consult an American dictionary.

on etymological and phonetic grounds, but this is not correct.

Some British usage guides claim that -ize is ‘ preferable’  in British English ɪf in doubt, remember that in British English -ise is almost always acceptable.

Note also analyse (AmE usually analyze) and paralyse (AmE  paralyze).

Capsize has -ize in both British and American English.

vocabulary •  347   doubling final consonants m:  slim -+ slimming /:        travel -+ travelling       t. sit -+ sitting gɪ big -+ bigger           r. prefer -+ preferred P' d:  sad -+ sadder           stop -+ stopped b:      rub -+ rubbing                                   n:  win -+ winnable We   double the following letters: 2 Which    consonants        are   doubled?

stop -+ stopped sit -+ sitting big -+ bigger -ing, -able, -y (or any other ending that begins with a vowel).

We   sometimes double the final consonant of a word before adding -ed, -er, -est, 1  doubling       before     vowels 347 doubling       final    consonants For words ending in -ie, ►    348.5. For adverbs ending in -ly, ►     345.

judg(e)ment and acknowledgement can be spelt with or without the -e after g.

(    charge -+ charging, face -+ facing) replace -+ replaceable courage -+ courageous In words that end with -ce or -ge, we  do not drop -e before a or o.
